formatDefinitions.displayName: Complete with words from the box

exercises.title: A: Open Cloze
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: When we receive unexpectedly wonderful news, we might suddenly burst (1) ...... in joy, unable to contain our excitement.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is phrasal verb, so the correct answer is "out".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Laughter appears to be a universal human response (2) ...... comic stimuli, observed consistently across all known cultures.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is response to something, so the correct answer is "to".

wrongOptions.2: The bare infinitive (2) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (2) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: We might feel (3) ...... laughing at the most inappropriate moment, or remain completely silent when everyone around us is amused.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is feel like doing, so the correct answer is "like".

wrongOptions.3: The bare infinitive (3) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: or even laugh (4) ...... no apparent reason whatsoever.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is for no reason, so the correct answer is "for".

wrongOptions.4: The bare infinitive (4) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Researchers are still investigating what lasting effects, if any, laughter has (5) ...... our physical and mental health.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is effect on health, so the correct answer is "on".

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (5) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: However, most people acknowledge the benefits (6) ...... a really good laugh, noting that it leaves them feeling more relaxed and positive, so it seems plausible that humour can help us to get (7) ...... challenging life events with far greater ease.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is benefits of, so the correct answer is "of".

wrongOptions.6: The bare infinitive (6) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: so it seems plausible that humour can help us to get (7) ...... challenging life events with far greater ease.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is overcome difficulties, so the correct answer is "over".

wrongOptions.7: The bare infinitive (7) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Phrases such as 'there's no point crying over (8) ...... milk'

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed idiom, so the correct answer is "spilt".

wrongOptions.8: The bare infinitive (8) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (8) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: and '(9) ...... and bear it' reflect traditional attitudes toward emotional expression that continue to vary widely across different societies.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is idiom, so the correct answer is "grin".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (9) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: If someone (10) ...... on a cheerful front while concealing their true feelings, we might say they are wearing a social mask.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is put on a front, so the correct answer is "put".

wrongOptions.10: The bare infinitive (10) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
That's the comedian the critics couldn't stand. **badly**
That's the comedian ...... the critics.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is go down badly, so the correct answer is "who went down badly with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "who went down badly with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
She was detained when officers found her opening the safe. **act**
Being ...... the safe, she was detained.
items.answer: caught in the act of opening
acceptedAnswers: caught in the act of opening

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is caught in the act, so the correct answer is "caught in the act of opening".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "caught in the act of opening", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
The quiet village we retired to is nothing like the busy capital where we were born. **cry**
The quiet village we retired to is ...... the busy capital where we were born.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very different, so the correct answer is "a far cry from".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "a far cry from", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The revised bus timetables will come into force on Monday. **effect**
The revised bus timetables will apply ...... Monday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is from a date, so the correct answer is "with effect from".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "with effect from", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I cannot fathom why anyone would ever choose to end their existence, can you? **own**
I cannot fathom why anyone would ever choose to ...... , can you?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is euphemism for suicide, so the correct answer is "take their own life".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
You're going to absolutely love the performance this evening! **treat**
You're ...... this evening!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is you'll enjoy it, so the correct answer is "in for a treat".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in for a treat", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Honestly, it was impossible not to laugh when Professor Clarke tripped over the projector cable. **face**
Honestly, ...... was impossible when Professor Clarke tripped over the projector cable.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is without laughing, so the correct answer is "with a straight face it".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "with a straight face it", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The neighbours of that family are furious about the building work. **arms**
That's the family ...... about the building work.
items.answer: whose neighbours are up in arms
acceptedAnswers: whose neighbours are up in arms

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very angry, so the correct answer is "whose neighbours are up in arms".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "whose neighbours are up in arms", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.title: C: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Try not to be so ...... (SENSE) . Can't you see that your comment has really upset Maria?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative adjective, so the correct answer is "insensitive".

wrongOptions.sense: The root SENSE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "insensitive".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of SENSE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: SENSE → insensitive: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "insensitive" is the word-formation answer from the root SENSE in this context.

items.sentence: My sister has found a ...... (HAND) little gadget for removing stickers from glass surfaces.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is useful, so the correct answer is "handy".

wrongOptions.hand: The root HAND cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "handy".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of HAND that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: HAND → handy: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "handy" is the word-formation answer from the root HAND in this context.

items.sentence: She worked tirelessly to ensure all her ...... (DEPEND) would remain financially secure after her retirement.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is people who depend, so the correct answer is "dependants".

wrongOptions.depend: The root DEPEND cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "dependants".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of DEPEND that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: DEPEND → dependants: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "dependants" is the word-formation answer from the root DEPEND in this context.

items.sentence: The head teacher made his ...... (APPROVE) of the students' conduct during the school trip abundantly clear.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is opposite of APPROVE, so the correct answer is "disapproval".

wrongOptions.approve: The root APPROVE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "disapproval".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of APPROVE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: APPROVE → disapproval: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "disapproval" is the word-formation answer from the root APPROVE in this context.

items.sentence: She doesn't consider herself a political ...... (ACT) , yet she campaigns vigorously for environmental causes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person who campaigns, so the correct answer is "activist".

wrongOptions.act: The root ACT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "activist".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ACT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ACT → activist: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "activist" is the word-formation answer from the root ACT in this context.

items.sentence: The conference organisers handed each participant a smart little ...... (FOLD) to keep all their printed materials together.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from FOLD, so the correct answer is "folder".

wrongOptions.fold: The root FOLD cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "folder".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of FOLD that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: FOLD → folder: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "folder" is the word-formation answer from the root FOLD in this context.

items.sentence: There is a known side-effect risk with this medication, but statistically speaking the probability is ...... (NEGLECT)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is so small it can be ignored, so the correct answer is "negligible".

wrongOptions.neglect: The root NEGLECT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "negligible".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of NEGLECT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: NEGLECT → negligible: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "negligible" is the word-formation answer from the root NEGLECT in this context.

items.sentence: After years of patient effort, the team were thrilled to see their ambitious project finally come to ...... (FRUIT)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is come to fruition, so the correct answer is "fruition".

wrongOptions.fruit: The root FRUIT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "fruition".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of FRUIT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "fruition" is the word-formation answer from the root FRUIT in this context.

exercises.title: D: Phrasal Verbs / Expressions
exercises.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap. There is one verb you will not need.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap. There is one verb you will not need.

items.sentence: I initially believed everything Sofia was telling us, but eventually I ...... on to the fact that she was winding everyone up.
items.explanation: Realise the truth → **cottoned** on to the fact.

items.sentence: After eating those strawberries, I've ...... out in an allergic rash across my arms.
items.explanation: Allergic reaction → **come** out in a rash.

items.sentence: The student nearly ...... out when she saw how difficult the entrance exam was going to be.
I'm afraid our beloved head of department ...... away peacefully after a long illness.
His family are keeping vigil at the hospital and praying he'll ...... through the night.
items.explanation: Recover from illness → **pull** through the night.

items.sentence: A sudden wave of dizziness came over me and then I completely ...... out.
Did people truly believe that burning certain herbs would ...... off evil spirits?
The old knee injury has been ...... up again since she returned to training.

items.sentenceBefore: She eventually gave up competitive swimming,

items.sentenceBefore: The company has been operating in the region for over two decades.
